risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "To promote comparability of the intervention and comparison clusters with respect to cluster characteristics, practitioners will be stratified according to whether they are urban or rural and block randomisation with random block sizes will be used within each stratum. The randomisation will be performed by a statistician not directly involved in the study and who is blinded to the identity of the practitioners." Comment: This describes a proper random allocation procedure (stratified block randomisation with random block sizes) and explicitly indicates random sequence generation, so the sequence generation is at low risk of bias.

Quote: "The randomisation will be performed by a statistician not directly involved in the study and who is blinded to the identity of the practitioners. Allocation of clusters to intervention or comparison will done following collection of baseline data. In other words, at the time of screening and recruitment of women, the allocation of GPs (and therefore, of women) will be unknown." Comment: Although this is a cluster trial, the paper explicitly states that allocation was done after baseline data collection and that the allocation was unknown during screening and recruitment, which indicates the recruiter could not foresee the upcoming assignment.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Due to the nature of the intervention (professional training plus patient counselling) it is not possible to blind the GPs to their status as intervention or control. Similarly, the immediate project team is not blind to GP participant status... women are not blinded..." Comment: Participants (women) and key personnel (GPs delivering the intervention) were explicitly not blinded, and the outcomes include subjective measures such as quality of life, mental health status, safety behaviours, readiness for change, comfort to discuss abuse, and attitudes/behaviours that could plausibly be influenced by knowledge of allocation; therefore the dominant performance-bias risk is high.

Quote: "There is no blinding as regards data collection based on the CONSORT guidelines [50], as the women and GPs themselves complete the surveys (i.e. data were not collected by a research assistant blinded to the allocation)." Comment: The outcome assessors were the women themselves (patient self-report) for most outcomes, and women were not blinded to group allocation. The trial also includes subjective outcomes such as quality of life, mental health status, safety behaviours, readiness for change, comfort discussing abuse, and satisfaction-type/process outcomes, all of which could be influenced by knowledge of allocation. Because an unblinded participant is the assessor for these self-reported outcomes, detection bias is high risk overall under the worst-outcome rule.

Summary: The paper is a trial protocol/design report and gives only anticipated follow-up loss, not actual attrition or per-arm outcome completion for the analysed time points. It states: "Following randomisation, approximately a third (88) will be lost to follow-up at 12 months ... leaving 89 women per group at 12 months." It also says analysis will be "intention-to-treat" and that the DMC will monitor "loss to follow-up," but there are no observed numbers of withdrawals, exclusions, or missing outcome data reported for results. Comment: Because this is only an expected attrition estimate and not evidence of realized incomplete outcome data, the risk of attrition bias cannot be judged confidently from the text alone.
